plain:
# Plain English Version

Someone handed me a letter. When I saw Captain Vernon's name mentioned, and heard that the letter was about something that interested me, I was ready for what it had to say. The letter said that Captain Vernon had just been put in charge of a ship called the _Daphne_. Now he could keep a promise he had made to my father, Dr Hawkesley, a long time ago. He was offering to let my father's son (me) come aboard the ship as a midshipman. The ship was going to work on the west coast of Africa. If I wanted to join, I had to get my supplies ready fast. The _Daphne_ was leaving in just four days from when the letter arrived. "Well, Dick, what do you think about Captain Vernon's offer?" my father asked me in a sad voice as I finished reading the letter and looked up at him. "Oh, father!" I said excitedly, "I _hope_ you'll let me go. Maybe I'll never get a chance like this again; and I"

Principal of the London Working Men's College; President of the London Chamber of Commerce; and Vice-Chairman of the London County Council With Numerous Illustrations London Macmillan and Co. and New York 1890 The Right of Translation and Reproduction is Reserved Richard Clay and Sons, Limited, London and Bungay. First Edition 1873. Reprinted 1874. New Edition 1890. PREFACE. For some years, much of my leisure time has been devoted to the study of the anatomy, development, and habits of the Annulosa, and especially of Insects, on which subjects I have published various memoirs, chiefly in the Transactions of the Royal, Linnæan, and Entomological Societies: of these papers I subjoin a list. Although the details, of which these memoirs necessarily for the most part consist, offer little interest, excepting to those persons who are specially devoted to Entomology, still there are portions which, having reference to the nature of metamorphoses and to the origin of insects, are of a more general character. I have also briefly referred to these questions in a Monograph of the Collembola and Thysanura, recently published by the Ray Society, and in the Opening Address to the Biological Section of the British Association
plain:
Bart., M.P., F.R.S., D.C.L., LL.D. is the Principal of the London Working Men's College. He is also President of the London Chamber of Commerce and Vice-Chairman of the London County Council. This book has many pictures. It was published in London by Macmillan and Co. and in New York in 1890. Richard Clay and Sons, Limited in London and Bungay own the rights to translate and reproduce this book. The first edition came out in 1873. It was reprinted in 1874. This is the new edition from 1890.

PREFACE

For several years, I have spent a lot of my free time studying how insects are built, how they grow, and how they behave. I have written several papers about these topics. Most of these papers were published in important science magazines. I have listed these papers at the end of this preface.

Most of these papers go into a lot of detail. This detail is only really interesting to people who study insects as their specialty. However, some parts of my work talk about bigger ideas. These include how insects change as they grow and where insects come from. These bigger ideas matter to more people.

I have also written briefly about these same ideas in a recent book about two types of tiny insects. This book was published by the Ray Society. I also discussed these ideas in a speech I gave to the science section of the British Association.

plain: This picture is from 1906 and appears at the front of this book. I'm not really happy about putting it there. It has nothing to do with the story in the pages that follow. The only reason it's here is because it's a real picture of me, the author who wrote this book and many others that people have liked. Also, I don't think my picture will interest my readers much. I've always believed that you learn more about an author from their writing than from looking at their face in a photograph. However, some newspapers and magazines have printed false and mean things about me in Britain, the colonies, and America. They've tried to turn my readers against me by spreading these lies. Because of this, my publishers think it's a good idea to put out a real, accurate picture of me so people can see what I actually look like today.

plain: A. Watson was born on January 18, 1854, in Salem, Massachusetts. He died on December 13, 1934, when he was over 80 years old. At age 13, he left school and started working in a store. He loved learning new things. Every new experience taught him something. He always wanted to get the most out of what he learned. This gave his life lots of different interests. In 1874, he got a job at an electrical shop owned by Charles Williams, Jr. The shop was at 109 Court Street in Boston. There he met Alexander Graham Bell. This is when his work with the telephone began. He wrote a book about this time in his life. In 1881, Watson had worked hard on telephone problems for many years. He was now rich and needed a break. George Routledge and Sons. This volume passed through several editions, and, in due course, was followed by a second series under the title of "More Bab Ballads," which achieved a popularity equal to that of its predecessor. Subsequently, excerpts were made from these two volumes, and, under the title of "Fifty Bab Ballads," had a very considerable sale; but I soon discovered that in making the selection for this volume I had discarded certain Ballads that were greater favourites with my readers than with me. Nevertheless this issue was followed by many editions, English and American, of "Bab Ballads," "More Bab Ballads," and "Fifty Bab Ballads," to the no little bewilderment of such of the public as had been good enough to concern themselves with my verses. So it became desirable (for our own private ends) that this confusion should be definitely cleared up; and thus it came to pass
plain: THE MACMILLAN COMPANY OF CANADA LIMITED _Toronto_ ST MARTIN'S PRESS INC _New York_ PRINTED IN GREAT BRITAIN AUTHOR'S NOTE About thirty years ago, I collected several of "The Bab Ballads." Most of these had been printed in a magazine called _Fun_. I published them with George Routledge and Sons. This book came out in several editions. Later, I made a second book called "More Bab Ballads." It was just as popular as the first one. Then I took pieces from both books and made a new book called "Fifty Bab Ballads." It sold really well. But I found out something. The ballads I left out were actually more popular with readers than the ones I chose. Still, after that book came out, there were many different editions. Some came out in England and some in America. There were copies of "Bab Ballads," "More Bab Ballads," and "Fifty Bab Ballads." This confused a lot of people who liked my work. So I decided to fix this mess and make things clear. That's why I'm doing this now.

plain: People have long believed that measuring light, color, lighting, and vision with tools and numbers is not enough. These measurements have helped us learn a lot. But they don't always match what we actually see with our eyes. In other words, there are many so-called optical illusions that we need to think about. All of them are interesting. Some are useful. Some we need to ignore. Scientists have written a lot about optical illusions from different angles, but much of what they've written disagrees with other scientists. Nobody has really looked at how optical illusions work in real life. There also isn't a book that covers this subject in a short, simple way but still covers everything. In other words, the goal has been to focus on what happens in real experiments.

plain: People have long believed that measuring light, color, lighting, and vision with scientific tools is not enough. You can't measure everything you actually see. Physical measurements have helped us learn a lot. But that doesn't mean they're perfect. In fact, what we measure with tools is often different from what we actually see with our eyes. This is why visual illusions exist. There are tons of them. Some are interesting. Some are useful. And some we should ignore. The goal is to focus on what actually happens.

plain:
This book is dedicated to Frances Mary Jenkins Olcott on January 25. She was someone whose kindness showed in her smile like flowers growing by sunny streams in May. But when she thought about other people's pain, her smile would fade into a gentle sadness.

FOREWORD

This book has over 200 stories about 23 important birthdays of the founders and builders of the countries in North and South America. The stories include more than 75 real historical people—men, women, and children. The stories are arranged by the school year, starting in October with Columbus. The book cover is decorated in George Washington's colors, red and white.

HOW TO TEACH HISTORY TO CHILDREN

These stories don't fill your head with boring facts and dates that put kids to sleep. Instead, they tell history in a way that interests boys and girls. What makes history real to children is learning about the strong personalities who shaped big world events. It's the powerful lives of the men themselves. It's how they prepared as kids for success later. It's their fights for what was right, their bravery, loyalty, and exciting adventures. It's also understanding why things happened and what caused them. This is what makes American history something children and young people actually care about.

plain:
This book is dedicated to Gladys Goddard. She has been a good friend to many boys and girls.

The author wants to thank Houghton, Mifflin and Company. They let her use a poem called "Vantage" by Josephine Preston Peabody. She also thanks Miss Margaret Sherwood for letting her use her poem "Indian Summer."

Books for girls can inspire them. Their real value is in how they spark ideas and feelings. Many different kinds of girls will read this story. But some girls really love good writing. This book includes those poems for those special readers.

Schools sometimes teach students that English literature is old. It belongs to the past, like Latin. But that's not true. There are great writers alive right now. They write beautiful things. Because they are alive, they can touch our hearts in ways that old, dead writers cannot.

If this book helps some girl find books like "Singing Leaves," or stories like "Daphne" and "King Sylvaine and Queen Aimée," then Catherine Smith and her friends will have done their job.

plain:
Dear Editor Whiz-Bang, Robbinsdale, Minnesota.

On September 27th, our train #12 stopped in Robbinsdale for 37 minutes to load what they said was 36,000 pounds of mail. We need to figure out how to handle this much mail without slowing down the trains too much. Can you please answer these questions for us?

First, how often do you publish the magazine? Second, what days or dates does it normally get mailed out? Third, about how much does each issue weigh, or how many copies do you send?

With this information, we can work out a special plan to get the magazines to the cities. We might leave a baggage car in Robbinsdale the day before the magazine needs to be sent out.

Yours truly,
M. J. Woulfe

The letter tells you everything! If all our Winter Annuals had been loaded at one time, Captain Billy could have filled an entire mail train by himself. From now on, dear reader, your news dealer will have Whiz Bang on the 15th of every month. Because we get so many orders, we will mail out a few truckloads every single day all month long. All the magazines will be held at the different post offices until

original: both these aspects of the Northern capital which is altogether absent from the sparkling beauty of the city on the Isis, and from the genius of the place. The impression that Oxford makes upon those who, familiar with her from early years, have learnt to know and love her in later life is remarkable. Teeming with much that is ancient, she appears the embodiment of youth and beauty. Exquisite in line, sparkling with light and colour, she seems ever bright and young, while her sons fall into decay and perish. "Alma Mater!" they cry, and love her for her loveliness, till their dim eyes can look on her no more. And this is for the reason that the true lovableness of Oxford cannot be learnt at once. As her charms have grown from age to age, so their real appreciation is gradual. Not that she cannot catch the eye of one who sees her for the first time, and, smiling, hold him captive. This she can do now and then; but even so her new lover has yet to learn her preciousness. It is worth while to try to understand what are the charms that have grown with her growth.
plain: The Northern capital has both of these qualities that you won't find in Oxford, which sits by the Isis River. Oxford makes a big impression on people who have known it since childhood and grown to love it as adults. It's full of old things, but it looks young and beautiful. It's gorgeous to look at, bright and colorful. It seems forever young while the people who went there get old and die. They call it "Alma Mater!" and love it for how beautiful it is until they can't see it anymore. The reason is that you can't understand what makes Oxford special right away. Its beauty has grown over hundreds of years, so you have to learn to appreciate it slowly. It's not that Oxford can't impress someone visiting for the first time. It can do that sometimes and make them fall in love with it. But even then, they still have a lot to learn about what makes it so precious. It's worth trying to figure out what makes Oxford special and how those special qualities came to be.

One can only sustain interest in a biography of the man by an ever-present sense that he is scarcely to be written about. All an author can do is, in few or many words, to put a conundrum to the reader--a conundrum that cannot even be stated in exciting terms. This apparition and wonder-worker of the eighteenth century, Franz Joseph Haydn, is compact of paradoxes and contradictions. Born a peasant, and remaining in thought and speech a peasant all his days, he became the friend of princes, dukes, and, generally speaking, very high society indeed--and this in days when class distinctions had to be observed. He effected a revolution in music, and revolutionists must have daring; and save in music he showed no sign of unusual daring. His shaping and handling of new forms called for high intellect, and he displayed no intellect whatever in any other way--nothing beyond a canny, cunning shrewdness. Until he was sixty his life was a plodding one of dull regularity and routine;
plain: Adults can read the good parts of a book and stop before it gets to the boring writing stuff. But Haydn is different. You can only stay interested in his biography if you remember that he's almost impossible to write about. All a writer can do is ask the reader a puzzle—a puzzle that can't even be described in an exciting way. Franz Joseph Haydn was a strange wonder-worker of the 1700s. He was full of contradictions. He was born poor and stayed that way in how he thought and spoke. But he became friends with princes, dukes, and really important people—and this was at a time when class differences really mattered. He changed music forever. People who change things have to be brave. But Haydn wasn't brave about anything except music. He created new types of music that required high intelligence. But he didn't show intelligence in any other way—just common sense and clever thinking. Until he was sixty, his life was boring and the same every single day.

Philadelphia, Published by George W. Jacobs & Co., 1900. This book is dedicated to my mother, Sarah Maxwell Lummis. She was the daughter of a man who fought in the American Revolution. I dedicate this work to her with respect and love.

INTRODUCTION

When Mrs. Ellet wrote her history called "The Women of the Revolution," she did not know how much people would care about Colonial and Revolutionary history. This interest became very strong in the last ten years of the 1800s. She also could not have guessed that women's groups would form across the country. These groups would become just as interested in the mothers of America as they were in the fathers of America. But the person who wrote these stories about famous women was ready for exactly this moment in American life. Her work now looks like she predicted the future. It also sums up what happened in the past. "The Women of the Revolution" came out in the middle of the century. plain: Salvia and other colorful annual flowers grew everywhere. Bright tulips stood tall near a large group of arum lilies that surrounded a small pool. Garth Macleod's goldfish swam alone in that pool. Roses grew all over the garden. Some were on stands in the flower beds, and others were trained along the wide porch. They formed a screen of beautiful flowers. The sweetness of the roses filled the whole garden. It was a hot spring afternoon. Tom Macleod was digging in a corner. He pushed his Panama hat back from his red face and stood up straight for a moment to stretch his sore back. Just then a car drove up to the gate and stopped. A short, heavy man hurried up the path. He waved at Tom, who was working across the lawn in his shirtsleeves. "Hello, Doctor!" Tom called out. "I'll see you soon, Tom," the doctor said. That was all. He disappeared behind the roses on the porch. Tom went back to work. But now there was a worried line between his eyes that hadn't been there before. He kept looking at the house, hoping to hear something. But no sound came. Finally, he moved quickly. He pushed his spade into the ground and walked with long strides toward the house.

plain: By William H. Russell, LL.D., Special Correspondent of the London Times. Boston: Gardner A. Fuller, No. 112 Washington Street. The Modern Age. I am presenting the first issue of the _Modern Age_ to readers. I chose Mr. Russell's letters because I think they are the best topic for right now. They deserve to be read by many people. Mr. Russell writes for the largest, best, and most powerful newspaper in the world. That shows his letters are good quality. They come to us with strong recommendations that we can trust. People everywhere want to understand what is happening in the South. This brilliant writing from Mr. Russell will give them plenty of answers. Mr. Russell covered the war in Crimea before this. That experience makes him perfect to write a fair and honest account of the Civil War in America. Issue two of the _Modern Age_ will have more of Mr. Russell's letters. After that, I will add popular speeches and sermons from our best religious leaders. The main goal of this work is to save the best ideas. We will keep them in an easy form. They come fresh and new.

The whole town adorns herself and attires herself like a bride for her wedding; the dark facades of marble and granite disappear beneath hangings of silk and festoons of flowers; the wealthy display their dazzling luxury, the poor drape themselves proudly in their rags. Everything is light, harmony, and perfume; the sound is like the hum of an immense hive, interrupted by a thousandfold outcry of joy impossible to describe. The bells repeat their sonorous sequences in every key; the arcades echo afar with the triumphal marches of military bands; the sellers of sherbet and water-melons sing out their deafening flourish from throats of copper. People form into groups; they meet, question, gesticulate; there are gleaming looks, eloquent gestures, picturesque attitudes; there is a general animation, an unknown charm, an indefinable intoxication. Earth is very near to heaven, and it is easy to understand that, if God were to banish death from this delightful spot, the Neapolitans would desire no other paradise. The story that we are about to tell opens with one of these magical pictures.
plain: The people here have simple, honest feelings. They believe in their own happiness so much that it's almost like poetry. The whole town gets dressed up like a bride on her wedding day. The dark stone buildings disappear under silk cloth and flower garlands. Rich people show off their expensive things. Poor people wear their old clothes with pride. Everything is bright, balanced, and smells good. The noise sounds like millions of bees, mixed with countless shouts of joy that you can't even describe. Bells ring out in every possible way. Military bands march through the covered walkways playing triumph songs. People selling drinks and watermelons yell really loud to sell their stuff. People gather in groups. They meet, ask questions, move their hands around a lot. There are bright eyes and meaningful hand gestures. Everything feels alive and exciting in a way that's hard to explain. It feels almost magical. Earth seems very close to heaven here. You can see why Neapolitans would think this place is paradise if God took away death. The story we're about to tell starts with one of these amazing moments.

plain: It's known all over the country. Girls are sent there from Chicago and New York, and even Boston. You'll be with the best girls, the nicest ones----" "That's exactly the problem," Honor said, sadly. "What do you mean?" "Girls. Just girls. Tons and tons of nothing but girls. Honestly, Muzzie, I don't think I could stand it." She was a big, solid teenager with a wide forehead and healthy pink skin and honest eyes. Her stepfather was sure she would never be as beautiful as her mother, but he was pretty sure she wouldn't need to be. He watched her carefully and found the way she acted really interesting. He laughed, and his wife looked shocked. "Stephen, dear! Don't encourage her to be strange. I don't like it when she's strange." Mrs. Lorimer was not strange herself, unless you thought it was strange to be stunningly beautiful and young-looking and charming at forty-one, with a second husband and six kids. She wasn't especially motherly except when she had to punish Honor, and then she always talked about herself in the third person. "Honor, Mother wants you to spend more time with girls." In these notes of foreign travel the object has been to cover a broad field without making a cumbersome volume, to do which, conciseness has necessarily been observed. In previous books the author has described much more in detail some of the countries here briefly spoken of. The volumes referred to are "Due-West; or, Round the World in Ten Months," and "Due-South; or, Cuba Past and Present," which were published by Houghton, Mifflin & Co., of Boston. Two other volumes, namely, "Due-North; or, Glimpses of Scandinavia and Russia," and "Under the Southern Cross; or, Travels in Australia and New Zealand," were issued by Ticknor & Co., of the same city. By the kind permission of both publishers, the author has felt at liberty to use his original notes in the preparation of these pages. It should be understood, however, that about one-half of the countries through which the reader is conducted in the present work are not mentioned in the volumes above referred to. The purpose has been to prepare a series of chapters adapted for youth, which, while affording pleasing entertainment, should also impart valuable information. plain: COOK, THE DISCOVERER.] PREFACE. In this book about traveling to foreign countries, I wanted to cover a lot of ground without making the book too long. To do that, I had to keep things short. In my other books, I wrote much more detail about some of the countries I talk about briefly here. Those books are called "Due-West; or, Round the World in Ten Months" and "Due-South; or, Cuba Past and Present." Houghton, Mifflin & Co. in Boston published those. Two more books I wrote are "Due-North; or, Glimpses of Scandinavia and Russia" and "Under the Southern Cross; or, Travels in Australia and New Zealand." Ticknor & Co. in Boston published those. Both publishers gave me permission to use my old notes to write this book. But you should know that about half of the countries in this book are not in those other books. My goal was to write chapters for young people. I wanted them to be fun to read and also teach you something important. It helps to use good maps while you read this.

original: living of late. Winifred was greatly surprised. She drew away her hand, half-angry, half-astonished. “Why is this?” she said. “Is it because it is so early that you forget”-- “It is because there is no longer any need of precaution,” he said very gravely, pressing her arm close to his side. She gazed at him with an incapacity to understand, which would have been incredible did it not happen so often at the great crises of life. “I don’t know what you mean; nothing is changed,” she said. “But you have not come to talk of you and me. Edward, how is my father?” She asked the question with scarcely a fear. Then suddenly looked in his face, flung his support from her, and flew upstairs without a word. The door of her father’s room was closed; she rushed at it breathless. It was half-opened after a little interval by old Hopkins, who barred the entrance. “You can’t come in yet, Miss Winifred, not yet,” he said, shaking his head. Hopkins was full of the solemn importance and excitement of one who has suddenly become an actor in a great event. He closed the door upon her as he spoke,
plain: Winifred was really surprised lately. She pulled her hand away. She was half angry and half shocked. "Why are you acting like this?" she asked. "Is it because it's so early that you're forgetting?" "It's because we don't need to be careful anymore," he said very seriously. He held her arm close to him. She looked at him but couldn't understand what he meant. This happened to her a lot during big moments in life. "I don't know what you're talking about. Nothing has changed," she said. "But you didn't come here to talk about us. Edward, how is my father?" She asked the question without much worry. Then suddenly she looked at his face. She let go of his arm and ran upstairs without saying anything. Her father's bedroom door was closed. She ran at it, out of breath. A moment later, old Hopkins opened it a little bit. He stood in the way. "You can't come in yet, Miss Winifred. Not yet," he said, shaking his head. Hopkins felt very important and excited. He was like someone who had suddenly become part of something big. He shut the door as he spoke.
